callouts.content: SKILL: /ccwork — The onboarding hub and lab runner. It loads lab exercises, creates tracking issues, and sets up starting branches. This is your entry point for every lab.
SKILL: /ccfold — Fetches the shared upstream CLAUDE.md template and merges it with local project customizations. One canonical template across all repos, with project-specific overrides preserved.
SKILL: /view — Opens a file in the system's default GUI viewer. Lets you inspect files visually outside the terminal, useful for reviewing code or documents before modifying them.

SKILL: /precheck — The pre-commit quality gate. Verifies branch/issue linkage, runs tests and lint, executes code review, and presents the mandatory checklist. Nothing gets committed without passing this.

SKILL: /scpmmr — Full pipeline in one command: Stage → Commit → Push → create MR → Merge. Despite being a single command, it doesn't skip any safety gates — precheck must pass and approval must be granted first.

This is what 'vanilla mode' looks like — Claude Code without a CLAUDE.md has no project context, no conventions, no guardrails. Everything that follows in this lab builds the scaffolding that makes the agent effective.

Dev-Team is project-level identity — it persists in CLAUDE.md across all sessions. Dev-Name and Dev-Avatar are session-level — a fresh identity each time. This separation lets the agent belong to a team while still being individually identifiable.

Watch the scoping negotiation here. The user proposed linting + convention updates. The agent assessed the project's existing tooling and recommended narrowing scope to just the lint script (since validate-lab.sh already existed). Good agents help scope work, not just execute it.

Notice: the agent creates an issue BEFORE writing any code. This is the Issue → Branch → Code → Review → Merge workflow. Skipping the issue means no traceability, no linked MR, and no way to close the loop. This rule is enforced by CLAUDE.md.

The pre-commit checklist is the quality gate. Every checkbox requires actual verification — not assumptions. 'Pre-commit passes' means the agent ran it, not 'it should pass.' This is one of the most important patterns in the workflow.

Auth failures are one of the most common blockers in real workflows. The agent hit a missing GitLab token, the session restarted twice, and the user had to provide the WE_GITLAB_TOKEN env var. This is realistic — not everything goes smoothly on the first try.
